food_subgroup: Grilled meat with legume dip and bread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for hummus chicken as a composite dish. FOODON_00002764 (chicken dish) used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as primary component anchor for this composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for hummus chicken as a composite dish. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) used as the primary defensible anchor; hummus component (chickpea-based dip) is a secondary component not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for hummus chicken composite dish as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 plate with chicken, hummus, and sides
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of grilled or roasted chicken (approximately 150–180 g), a generous scoop of hummus (approximately 80–100 g), pita bread (approximately 80 g), and a fresh side salad (approximately 80–100 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy and Foundation Foods: Chicken (broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted); Hummus, commercial; Pita bread, white, enriched; Raw salad vegetables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for hummus chicken as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA SR Legacy entries: grilled chicken breast (~40% by weight), commercial hummus (~22%), white pita bread (~18%), and fresh salad vegetables (~20%). The input per_100g values (energy 166.67 kcal, protein 12.22 g, fat 8.89 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g) are consistent with this weighted blend and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients set to -1 where USDA component-level data for this specific blend is not directly available as a single composite entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; chickpea-based hummus and mixed grain/legume composite analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hummus chicken as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: hummus/chickpeas (GI ~28–36, University of Sydney GI Database) and white pita bread (GI ~57, University of Sydney GI Database), weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution (~55% from chickpeas/hummus, ~45% from pita bread). Weighted GI = (0.55 × 32) + (0.45 × 57) ≈ 43, rounded to 45. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 45 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 = 20.25.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 266.67 mg/100 g (approximately 1,200 mg per 450 g serving) is in the borderline-to-elevated range for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt during preparation, choose wholemeal pita to reduce refined carbohydrate load, and limit tahini quantity; the potassium contribution from chickpeas, cucumber, and tomatoes (~310 mg/100 g estimated) partially offsets sodium impact, but total serving sodium should be monitored against a 1,500–2,000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) driven by chickpea-based hummus, high protein (12.22 g/100 g), and dietary fibre (2.22 g/100 g) supporting postprandial glucose control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hummus chicken is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI (~45), high protein content from grilled chicken and chickpeas, and fibre from chickpeas and salad vegetables; patients should request wholemeal pita or reduce pita portion to half to further lower the glycemic load, and monitor total carbohydrate intake per serving (~45 g per 450 g plate).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.78 g/100 g), heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil and tahini, and anti-inflammatory components from chickpeas and garlic
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hummus chicken is broadly suitable for cardiovascular disease management given its low saturated fat content (1.78 g/100 g), absence of significant trans fat (0.02 g/100 g), and the presence of olive oil-derived MUFAs and chickpea fibre which support LDL reduction; sodium at 266.67 mg/100 g warrants portion awareness, and patients should request grilled rather than fried chicken preparation to avoid additional saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFAs from olive oil, polyphenols from garlic and cumin, and low saturated fat (1.78 g/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hummus chicken is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to olive oil's oleocanthal content, chickpea fibre supporting gut microbiome immune regulation, and low saturated fat; patients sensitive to nightshades should monitor tolerance to tomatoes in the salad component, and extra-virgin olive oil should be specified to maximise anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hummus Chicken is a Middle Eastern-inspired composite dish, popular in Turkish cuisine and widely available in Singapore's food delivery market, consisting of grilled or roasted chicken served alongside a generous portion of creamy hummus, warm pita bread, and a fresh side salad of cucumber, tomato, and mixed greens. The chicken is typically marinated with cumin, paprika, garlic, and olive oil before grilling or roasting, yielding a smoky, savoury flavour with a tender, juicy texture. The hummus is prepared by blending cooked chickpeas with tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil into a smooth, nutty, slightly tangy dip. The dish delivers a balanced macronutrient profile per 100 g: approximately 167 kcal, 12.2 g protein, 8.9 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from olive oil and tahini), and 10 g carbohydrate, with 2.2 g dietary fibre and a low glycemic index of approximately 45 driven by the chickpea component. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI and high protein-to-carbohydrate ratio; it is broadly cardiovascular-friendly due to low saturated fat and heart-healthy MUFAs; sodium at 266.67 mg/100 g requires monitoring for hypertension patients. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore food service contexts and contains sesame (tahini) and gluten (pita bread) as primary allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA SR Legacy component entries (grilled chicken breast, commercial hummus, white pita bread, fresh salad vegetables) as no single approved-source entry exists for hummus chicken as a composite dish. The input values from the grabfood source are consistent with this USDA-derived estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for chickpeas/hummus and white pita bread. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact composite dish entry exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component blend: grilled chicken breast (~40%), commercial hummus (~22%), white pita bread (~18%), fresh salad vegetables (~20%) by weight. Input grabfood values are consistent with this estimate and retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full mineral panel, and moisture data for this specific hummus chicken composite dish are not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual component data exists but a validated composite blend value is not published. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate weighted from University of Sydney GI Database values for hummus/chickpeas (GI ~32) and white pita bread (GI ~57) based on approximate carbohydrate contribution ratios. 4. Ontology anchored to USDA FoodData Central chicken dish and FoodEx2 A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) as primary defensible anchors; no specific composite hummus chicken code exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2. 5. Dish is sold in Singapore via food delivery platforms under Turkish/Middle Eastern cuisine category; country_of_origin recorded as Singapore (point of sale) with Turkish cultural origin. 6. All aliases_by_language string values verified present in flat aliases array.
